_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, evaluation findings, indication for procedure, and planned line of treatment?
Did history, physical examination, and radiological investigations confirm the diagnosis?
Was clinical presentation, grading and radiological investigations indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details provided?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the imaging indicative of surgery?
Was post-op CT Brain report submitted?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge submitted?

common_queries: Clinical notes lack detailed history, signs/symptoms, evaluation findings, indication for procedure, or planned line of treatment.
History, physical examination, or radiological investigations did not confirm the diagnosis.
CT/MRI Brain report not submitted at pre-authorization stage.
Histopathological Examination report not submitted at claim submission.
Clinical presentation, grading, or radiological investigations were not indicative of surgery.
Post-op CT Brain report not submitted at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Meningioma is the commonest primary central nervous system tumor accounting for about 37.6% of them; and approximately 50% of all benign brain tumors.
Meningioma originates from the meningeal layers of either the brain or the spinal cord. Meningioma commonly originates from meningothelial arachnoid cap cells.
Most of the meningiomas are sporadic, benign, and slowly growing.
Most intracranial meningiomas are located supratentorial. They are commonly seen in the parasagittal, brain convexity, sphenoid ridge, anterior and posterior parafalcine areas, and olfactory groove.
Other reported locations of intracranial meningiomas include suprasellar, posterior fossa (cerebellopontine), intraventricular, and intra-orbital areas.
Several predisposing factors increase the risk of occurrence, including genetic disorders such as neurofibromatosis type 2, exposure to radiation, hormonal therapy, and family history.
Classification (according to WHO): Grade I typical meningiomas are low grade tumors and are the most common (slowly growing cells).
Classification (according to WHO): Grade II atypical meningiomas are mid-grade tumors (higher chance of recurrence); subtypes include choroid and clear cell meningioma.
Classification (according to WHO): Grade III anaplastic meningiomas are malignant (cancerous, fast-growing); subtypes include papillary and rhabdoid meningioma.
Clinical Manifestation: Dependent on location and size.
Many small meningiomas are found incidentally and are entirely asymptomatic.
Most common presentations include: headache, paresis, visual impairments, anosmia, neurological deficits.

Meningiomas may also become clinically apparent due to mass effect depending on their location: Supratentorial (Parasagittal, convexities - seizures and hemiparesis; Sphenoid ridge; Olfactory groove/planum sphenoidale - anosmia, foster kennedy syndrome; Juxtasellar - visual field defects, cranial nerve deficits); Infratentorial (Obstructive hydrocephalus, Cranial nerve deficits); Miscellaneous intradural (<5% - Intraventricular meningioma, Optic nerve meningioma, Pineal gland - parinaud syndrome, obstructive hydrocephalus).
surgical_options: Observation: Asymptomatic small-sized tumors, and patients with cavernous sinus meningiomas, can be followed up with close observation annually or biennially, including serial imaging (brain MRI).

Surgery: Symptomatic lesions and those with accelerated growth are primarily treated with maximum gross total surgical resection. Simpson grade correlates surgical resection completeness with symptomatic recurrence.

Radiation: External beam radiotherapy or brachytherapy after surgical resection for grade II and III meningiomas. Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) for patients unfit for surgery, skull base meningiomas, recurrent, or incompletely resected meningiomas.
Chemotherapy: Adjuvant therapies to reduce recurrence rate in incompletely removed, atypical, or malignant meningiomas.
complications: Non-surgical: risk of recurrence, increase in size, accelerated growth. Calcification, progression to grade 2 or 3, malignancy, brain invasion, and rarely metastasis.

Surgical and medical: hematoma, infection, venous thrombosis, CSF leak, injury to surrounding anatomical structures, worsening of neurological deficits, aspiration pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, myocardial ischemia, stroke.
Post radiosurgery: risk of cranial nerve deficits (e.g., optic nerve injury in intracranial meningioma), increasing risk of toxicity due to radiotherapy.
